description: What incident response interviews test: ability to contain, investigate, and recover from security incidents. Tests both procedural knowledge (NIST/SANS frameworks) and hands-on technical depth (how you actually triage, hunt, and write reports).

junior.label: Junior / Associate
characteristics: Knows the phases of incident response by name but cannot explain the decision logic between phases
Describes IR process as linear and sequential rather than iterative
Relies exclusively on automated alerts; does not proactively hunt for undetected activity
Cannot articulate the difference between an incident and an event without prompting
Identifies indicators from pre-existing detection rules but cannot generate new hypotheses
Refers to 'looking at logs' without specifying which logs, which fields, or which time window
Scoping is tool-driven: 'I run a Splunk search' rather than thinking about attacker objectives
Containment strategy defaults to immediately isolating machines without considering business impact
Cannot explain why you would NOT isolate an endpoint during some investigations
Documentation is an afterthought; does not mention chain of custody or evidence integrity
Confuses eradication with containment
Uses vague language: 'malicious activity,' 'suspicious behavior' without specifics
Knows Splunk or a SIEM by name but cannot write a query from scratch
Familiarity with IOCs (hashes, IPs) but no understanding of IOC decay or pivoting
Cannot describe a post-incident review process or what lessons learned actually produces
example_phrases: I would escalate to my manager and we would follow the playbook
I'd look at the SIEM to see if there were any alerts
We'd isolate the infected machine so it can't spread
I'd check if the antivirus found anything
I know the IR phases are preparation, identification, containment, eradication, recovery, and lessons learned
I would document everything in a ticket
We'd reset the user's password to be safe
I would check VirusTotal for the hash
red_flags: Claims 'senior' experience but cannot name specific Event IDs for common attacks
Says 'I've handled many incidents' but cannot walk through a single one with technical specificity
Knows NIST SP 800-61 by name but cannot describe what's actually in it
Describes IR only from a management perspective when asked for technical detail
Mentions 'using a runbook' as an answer to every technical question
Cannot explain why timestamps matter or how log timestamps can be manipulated
Thinks all incidents require full disk imaging before any analysis can begin
expected_frameworks: Basic MITRE ATT&CK awareness (knows it exists)

mid.label: Mid-Level
characteristics: Can triage an alert and immediately ask scoping questions: blast radius, patient zero, initial vector
Writes functional SIEM queries without help; filters by field, time, and specific event IDs
Knows specific Windows Event IDs: 4624, 4625, 4648, 4688, 4698, 4672 and what each proves
Can explain logon types (2=interactive, 3=network, 10=remote interactive) and why they matter
Understands difference between scheduled task persistence (4698) and service persistence (4697)
Pivots between artifacts: process creation leads to network connections leads to DNS queries
Uses MITRE ATT&CK to map observed behaviors to techniques, not just as a checklist
Can explain why you collect volatile data (memory, running processes, network connections) before disk
Knows the 'order of volatility' and applies it during live response
Understands containment tradeoffs: network isolation vs. port blocking vs. account disable
Has performed phishing investigations: header analysis, URL detonation, attachment sandbox
Can extract useful data from a PowerShell transcript log or 4104 (script block logging) event
Familiar with common attacker tools: PsExec, Cobalt Strike, Mimikatz, and their forensic artifacts
Knows what a C2 beacon looks like in network traffic (beaconing intervals, JA3/JA3S)
Can write a basic incident timeline and communicate findings to non-technical stakeholders
Understands basic malware persistence: registry run keys, scheduled tasks, services, WMI subscriptions
example_phrases: First I want to understand scope: how many systems, is this still active, and what's the potential blast radius
I'd pivot from the initial alert by looking at 4688 events around the same time window on that host
Before isolating I'd capture volatile data - running processes, network connections, memory if possible
The PowerShell logging showed the encoded command, I decoded it and it was a stager calling out to a C2
I mapped the attacker's actions to MITRE - initial access was T1566.001, they moved laterally via T1021.002
The beaconing interval was suspiciously regular, every 300 seconds, which is a Cobalt Strike default
I correlated the 4624 type 3 logon with a 5140 share access event to confirm lateral movement
red_flags: Talks about MITRE ATT&CK mapping but cannot map a specific observed behavior to a technique ID
Says 'I'd image the disk' as the first response to an active intrusion on a production server
Cannot explain the difference between persistence via run key vs. scheduled task vs. service
Confuses Sigma rules with YARA rules
Claims to do threat hunting but describes it as reviewing existing SIEM alerts
Mentions JA3/JA3S without being able to explain what it is
expected_tools: CrowdStrike Falcon / SentinelOne / Defender for Endpoint
Velociraptor or KAPE for endpoint collection
MISP or similar for IOC management
expected_frameworks: NIST SP 800-61 (practical application, not just phases)
Lockheed Martin Cyber Kill Chain (for attacker lifecycle)

senior.label: Senior
characteristics: Can scope an entire intrusion from a single alert by reasoning about attacker objectives and likely pivots
Builds incident response playbooks that account for edge cases and failure modes, not just happy paths
Leads tabletop exercises and can identify where existing playbooks will break under real conditions
Writes detection content from IR findings: turns IOCs and TTPs into durable SIEM/EDR detections
Can distinguish between a nation-state intrusion, ransomware operator, and opportunistic script kiddie from artifact patterns alone
Understands advanced attacker techniques: living-off-the-land (LOLBins), fileless malware, BYOVD
Can hunt for C2 via behavioral indicators (beaconing, DNS tunneling, unusual parent-child process trees)
Performs memory forensics beyond basics: heap scanning, finding injected code, identifying hooks
Has managed multi-system intrusions and knows how to coordinate investigation across teams
Understands log source gaps and knows what an attacker can do that won't appear in standard telemetry
Can recover deleted forensic artifacts using $MFT, VSS, or Windows Shadow Copies
Communicates the business risk of an incident in terms executives understand
Can scope for data exfiltration: knows where staging directories appear, what DLP logs show, how to detect staged ZIP archives
Has performed cloud incident response (AWS CloudTrail, Azure Activity Log, GCP Audit Logs)
Understands legal and regulatory notification requirements during an incident (GDPR 72hr, SEC 4-day)
Can write forensically sound containment strategies that preserve evidence integrity
example_phrases: The attacker used WMI subscriptions for persistence - that's subtle because most playbooks only check registry run keys and scheduled tasks
I look at the parent-child process tree first. When I see Word spawning PowerShell, that's the story right there
We scoped it by looking at lateral movement artifacts across all DCs - 4769 Kerberos tickets for sensitive systems from the compromised account
We found the attacker had disabled Windows Defender's real-time protection via Group Policy three days before the ransomware deployed
The exfil happened over HTTPS to a legitimate cloud storage provider - we found it via unusual outbound data volume on the DLP logs, not the firewall
I correlated memory artifacts from Volatility with disk artifacts from KAPE - the in-memory process had no on-disk counterpart, which confirmed process injection
Before we issued the containment order, we made sure we'd already extracted enough evidence to reconstruct the timeline - you only get one shot at live data
red_flags: Focuses only on technical remediation without mentioning stakeholder communication or legal requirements
Has never led or built a playbook from scratch
Cannot explain BYOVD (Bring Your Own Vulnerable Driver) or why it defeats EDR
Describes 'advanced' hunting as searching for known bad hashes
Has no opinion on which detection gaps concern them most in their previous environment
expected_tools: Cloud-native tools: CloudTrail Insights, Defender for Cloud
expected_frameworks: NIST SP 800-61 (contributing to, not just following)
IR retainer and third-party coordination experience
Chain of custody and legal hold procedures

staff.label: Staff / Principal
characteristics: Designs the IR program: defines retainer strategy, escalation matrix, communication trees, legal counsel integration
Measures IR effectiveness with metrics: MTTD, MTTR, detection coverage by MITRE technique, recurrence rate
Has opinions on IR platform architecture: SOAR vs. SIEM-native vs. case management, and articulates the tradeoffs
Drives post-incident review process that results in measurable improvements, not just lessons-learned documents
Can evaluate and select IR vendors: knows what questions to ask a retainer firm in an RFP
Has managed IR during regulatory investigations or legal proceedings
Understands incident classification taxonomies (VERIS, ENISA, CISA incident severity) and why they matter for reporting
Has responded to supply chain attacks, insider threats, and cloud-native attacks as distinct categories with distinct playbooks
Designs chaos engineering exercises and red team engagements specifically to stress-test IR capabilities
Can speak to IR maturity models and where an organization sits on the capability spectrum
Has contributed to public IR knowledge: threat intelligence sharing (ISACs, STIX/TAXII), public disclosures, industry reports
Understands systemic organizational failures that enable incidents: identity hygiene, patching cadence, architecture decisions
Has managed crisis communication during a major incident: coordinating with PR, legal, executives, and regulators simultaneously
Knows when to call in a third party vs. handle in-house and has managed those handoffs
Can design IR automation (SOAR playbooks, orchestration) that reduces toil without creating automation debt
example_phrases: Our MTTD was 4 days for a sophisticated intrusion - after the post-incident review, we redesigned our detection around lateral movement rather than initial access, and got it to under 8 hours
The retainer agreement needs to address data jurisdiction clauses before you need it - I've seen incident response stall because the vendor couldn't legally process EU data
We built the IR program around VERIS for classification so we could actually trend our incident types year-over-year and justify budget
The question isn't whether to automate containment - it's which containment actions are safe to automate and which require human judgment. Getting that wrong is worse than not automating
I had to brief the board at 2am with an honest assessment of breach scope - the discipline is knowing the difference between what you know, what you suspect, and what you don't know yet
After the supply chain incident we redesigned our detection to focus on anomalous behavior from trusted software, not just known-bad IOCs
We contributed our TTPs to the relevant ISAC under the TLP framework so other organizations could defend against the same threat actor
red_flags: Has no metrics for IR program effectiveness
Cannot describe what 'good' looks like for MTTD or MTTR in their sector
Has never designed a playbook from scratch, only followed existing ones
Conflates IR program maturity with the sophistication of individual analysts
Cannot explain VERIS or why incident classification matters beyond ticketing
expected_tools: SOAR platforms (Palo Alto XSOAR, Splunk SOAR, Microsoft Sentinel Automation)
Threat intelligence platforms (MISP, Anomali, Recorded Future)
GRC and case management integration (ServiceNow, Archer)
Cloud-native security: AWS Security Hub, Azure Sentinel, GCP Chronicle
expected_frameworks: VERIS (Vocabulary for Event Recording and Incident Sharing)
Legal frameworks: GDPR Article 33, SEC cybersecurity disclosure rules, HIPAA Breach Notification

positive: Immediately asks scoping questions before describing actions
Speaks in specifics: exact Event IDs, exact tool names, exact field names
Describes incidents they personally investigated with specific technical details
Acknowledges what they didn't know and how they figured it out
Considers attacker perspective when designing investigation approach
Discusses tradeoffs between actions (e.g., isolation vs. evidence preservation)
Mentions documentation and chain of custody without being prompted
References a specific failure or lesson they learned from an incident
Connects tactical finding to strategic attacker objective
negative: Describes IR process purely from a procedural/compliance perspective with no technical depth
Uses vague language when asked for specifics ('I'd look at the logs')
Cannot recall a specific incident they personally worked
Treats MITRE ATT&CK as a checkbox compliance exercise
Describes all incidents as following the same linear playbook
Never mentions business impact or stakeholder communication
Immediate answer to every containment question is 'isolate the machine'
Cannot explain why a specific artifact proves a specific attacker action
